rule: Chewing gum is banned in Singapore. Do not bring or buy gum.
No eating or drinking on MRT trains — S$500 fine.
Jaywalking is a fineable offence (S$50). Always use pedestrian crossings.
Durian fruit is banned in most hotels and on public transport.
Tipping is not expected in Singapore — service charge is included in bills.
Smoking in public places is prohibited. Designated smoking areas only.
Never tip in Japan — it can be considered rude or confusing.
Do not talk on the phone on trains. Set devices to manner mode (silent).
Remove shoes before entering temples, shrines, and many traditional restaurants.
Do not eat or drink while walking — it is considered impolite.
Bow slightly when greeting people. A 15-degree bow is sufficient for tourists.
Carrying over ¥1 million in cash into Japan must be declared at customs.
Tattoos may prevent entry to public baths (onsen) and some gyms.
Stand on the right side of escalators on the London Underground.
Tipping 10-15% at restaurants is customary but not mandatory.
Queue properly. Cutting in line is a serious social faux pas.
The Tube generally stops around midnight. Check Night Tube schedule for weekends.
Remove shoes before entering temples, mosques, and gurdwaras.
Dress modestly at religious sites — cover shoulders and knees.
Use your right hand for eating and greeting — left hand is considered unclean.
Photography may be restricted inside some temples and museums. Always ask.
Bargaining is expected at markets and street stalls, not at malls.
PDA (public display of affection) is frowned upon in many areas.
Tip 18-20% at restaurants — it is expected, not optional.
Jaywalking tickets are enforced in some cities (especially LA).
Carry photo ID (passport) at all times — it may be requested at bars/hotels.
Tax is not included in listed prices — expect ~7-10% added at checkout.
Public displays of affection are illegal in the UAE. Even holding hands may attract attention.
Alcohol consumption is only permitted in licensed venues (hotels, restaurants with license).
Dress conservatively outside beach/pool areas. Cover shoulders and knees at mosques.
Swearing, rude gestures, and loud behaviour in public can result in fines or arrest.
It is illegal to criticise or disrespect the Thai royal family (lèse-majesté law).
Always remove shoes before entering temples. Dress modestly — no tank tops or shorts.
Avoid pointing at people or things with your feet — feet are considered the lowest part of the body.
Do not touch anyone's head — the head is considered sacred.
